questions.question: What does the Acevedo study (2012) show about brain activity in long-term satisfying relationships?

options.text: Complete loss of all dopamine reward activity after 2 years
Continued dopamine reward pathway activation (VTA, caudate) PLUS increased oxytocin/opioid-rich region activity — addition of calm bonding to continued passion
Long-term relationships show no brain activity at all
Only unhappy long-term relationships show brain activity

questions.explanation: The Acevedo study scanned individuals in relationships averaging 21 years who reported being 'madly in love.' They showed continued activation of dopamine reward regions (VTA, caudate) when viewing partner photos PLUS increased activity in oxytocin/opioid-receptor-rich regions associated with attachment and calm. This is not loss of passion but ADDITION of security to continued reward activity. Long-term love = dopamine + calming bonding, not replacement. This was true for satisfying relationships; distressed relationships didn't show this pattern.

questions.question: What is the difference between healthy transition to long-term bonding vs. falling out of love?

options.text: There is no difference — any reduction in intensity means falling out of love
Healthy transition = reduced obsessive intensity but continued security/connection/admiration; Falling out of love = loss of both intensity AND connection (contempt, avoidance, resentment)
Healthy transition means no longer caring about your partner at all
Falling out of love is impossible — all relationships maintain early intensity forever

questions.explanation: Healthy transition involves ADDING security (calm, trust, deep connection) while losing obsessive intensity (normalized serotonin/norepinephrine). You shift from 'I can't stop thinking about them' to 'I feel deeply secure and connected.' Falling out of love involves losing BOTH intensity AND connection: persistent contempt, avoidance of intimacy, frequent thoughts of leaving, absence of positive emotions, resentment dominating. The key: healthy transition maintains connection; falling out loses it entirely.

questions.question: What features emerge in long-term bonding that cannot exist in early relationships?

options.text: Nothing positive — long-term relationships only involve loss
Deep security from years of consistent behavior, co-regulation (partners stabilize each other's emotions), shared meaning and rituals, confidence from weathering adversity together
Only boredom and routine
Long-term relationships are identical to early relationships

questions.explanation: Long-term bonding adds features impossible in early relationships: deep security (confidence in partner's reliability built over years), co-regulation (partners' emotional states synchronize and stabilize each other), shared meaning (rituals, traditions, inside jokes, joint identity), weathering adversity together (surviving loss, illness, stress builds resilience). These require time, history, and accumulated experiences. They are the adaptive trade-off for reduced obsessive intensity — you gain depth and security.

questions.question: When should you seek couples therapy for long-term relationship concerns?

options.text: Immediately upon any reduction in early intensity
When persistent contempt/resentment, complete loss of sexual desire, chronic emotional avoidance, or frequent thoughts of leaving don't improve with communication or self-help efforts
Never — all long-term relationships should handle problems independently
Only if both partners have diagnosed disorders

questions.explanation: Couples therapy (EFT, Gottman Method) is appropriate when: persistent contempt/resentment doesn't resolve with communication, complete loss of sexual desire (not just reduced frequency), chronic emotional or physical avoidance, frequent ideation about leaving, absence of positive emotions — and these patterns don't improve with honest communication or self-help. Temporary stress-related distance is normal; persistent patterns impairing connection warrant professional help. Therapy isn't needed for normal transition to long-term bonding — it's needed when connection is genuinely lost.

questions.question: What evidence-based strategies help maintain connection in long-term relationships?

options.text: Avoid all novelty and rely entirely on routine
Novel shared activities, expressed appreciation/admiration, physical/emotional intimacy, skillful conflict management and repair, balancing autonomy and interdependence
Never communicate about problems — just hope they resolve on their own
Spend all time together to avoid any independence

questions.explanation: Evidence-based maintenance strategies: (1) Novelty and shared challenges (new activities boost dopamine and satisfaction), (2) Expressed appreciation/admiration (Gottman's research on positive-to-negative ratio), (3) Physical intimacy and non-sexual affection (oxytocin bonding), (4) Emotional intimacy and vulnerability (sharing fears/dreams, responding with empathy), (5) Conflict management and repair (de-escalate skillfully, return to connection after ruptures), (6) Separate identities and interdependence (maintain individual friendships/hobbies while supporting partner). Balance is key: routine alone is comforting but doesn't activate reward; enmeshment and parallel lives are both problematic.

title: Long-Term Love — From Intensity to Security - Quiz
